item.title: You guys saw my first Comicon, I put more effort in for the second
item.description:
This was at MCM Comicon in London, at the Excel Arena
The Comicon squad I attended with…not what you’d call a particularly coordinated group of costumes
Obligatory superhero landing! Thanks for getting this far through the dump, I know it’s a lot of essentially the same photos, but I was excited to show you guys! Your feedback on the last post gave me the nerve to take it one step further. I appreciate you
item.dc:creator: LastSunnyDofKrypton

item.title: LDS Mormon church PR employees about to get fired.
item.description:
June is almost here! LDS PR is about to get their temple recommends revoked. In my work to expose the LDS Mormon Church for the racist, misogynistic, transphobic, real estate company that it is, I have a lot of Mormon apologists engage with me. They tell me they are “Mormon and not homophobic”. But, your tithing dollars support LGBTQIA hate. The Proclamation of the FaMiLy is a homophobic, transphobic piece of crap doctrine. My estranged TBM parents have it framed (from Deseret, $500 prolly). It’s hanging in a prominent area of their house. My elderly Mormon parents have three biological grandchildren. One of them is trans. They cast the child out. My spouse and I are a safe place for the child who is still figuring out who they are. I can’t imagine the pain of having your grandparents estrange you for simply being who you are. LDS Mormons, put your money where your mouth is and use the logo in June. Use any LDS themed PRIDE logo. Withhold your tithing until the Church updates its doctrine to not be homophobic. It could be fun! Source: https://www.churchofjesuschrist.org/study/scriptures/the-family-a-proclamation-to-the-world/the-family-a-proclamation-to-the-world?lang=eng
#2
item.dc:creator: lurkyloos

item.title: Three weeks no alcohol
item.description: I still continue to be somewhat impressed with myself. I thought 2 weeks would be a real milestone and now I think I'm heading to a month because so far I am doing it. I lost quite a bit of weight even though I'm not that big of a guy. I guess it was all those worthless empty calories. I sleep a lot better. My blood pressure is down. I feel more relaxed. The only bad thing is eating large quantities of candy or chocolate bars.....arg. Ps: thanks for the kind comments!
item.dc:creator: oldradios19772000

item.title: Down from 300lbs to 195lbs
item.description: It's been quite the experience of being nicotine free after 21 years of smoking and vaping for the last 2 years. Shit well, my friends! (originally posted by @TasteOfNoise on 2023-05-27 16:41:46)
item.dc:creator: ThisTimeLastYear

item.title: Crank Cash Register Drawer & Marble Repair - Mini-Update #3!
item.description:
Hey folks! Tiny update on the cash register this weekend, then it's off to shitpost and play some 7 Days to Die! I spent a few hours yesterday and today tackling one of the confounding little problems this register has, namely the cash drawer and its broken and missing hardware.
First thing the owner told me when I picked it up was that the drawer wouldn't close. Well, imagine that, if you remove all the fasteners from the mechanism that latches into the machine it'll probably not stay shut for very long. :D Sarcasm aside, the back of this drawer needs attention. Since I happened to have some donor machines downstairs, I figured I'd start easy and replace that busted spring.
This is the first time I've encountered a broken spring in a project, but to be fair I've done less than ten registers so for all I know this is common. Either way this register saw a lot of use right up until the day I bought it, so something was bound to wear out.
Luckily I have spares! For now, a trimmed down ugly duckling is the best I can do. Mechanically it'll do its job, though it's not a forever fix. Both springs feel as if they have the same amount of resistance, so I'm not worried about one working harder than the other. When I have time I'll start checking with other collectors for a matching pair, or just see if McMaster-Carr can't help me out. Anyhoo, with that box ticked, I can turn my attention to the latching hardware that literally fell off the back of the drawer.
In an earlier update I mentioned that I thought all three screw holes were from wood screws, and that they must have been torn out of the wood at some point. As it turns out, only the centermost hole held a wood screw while the left and right accommodated a set of bolts to secure the mechanism to the drawer. Easier work for me, I supposed! A lot of you suggested that I use toothpicks and wood glue to fill in the hole so I could tap a new screw. I thought about it, but I ended up taking the advice of a woodworking Imgurian who suggested drilling the hole out and gluing in a solid dowel instead. Sooo that's what I did!
I drilled the hole out to a quarter inch, picked up a section of quarter inch oak dowel, and tapped it in yesterday. This morning everything had set up nicely and I used a chisel to cut away the excess.
I was sure I would carve up the surrounding wood, but it turns out chisels have a handy flat bottom that make flush cuts really easy. It's like they planned it that way or something!
While I was at the hardware store I measured up some new fasteners as well.
They match the holes in the hardware better than the holes in the wood. :)
Had to do a little bit of adjusting to make sure everything snugged up flush to the edge of the drawer. Hand tightened them with a socket and screwdriver.
Heck, I even made sure to get flanged bolts so the wood wouldn't splinter. I can be smart sometimes!
Finished up with the missing wood screw. For those of you who are curious, I'm pretty sure they use a wood screw in the center slot because of the wooden divider on the opposite side of that board. I figure the choice came down to aesthetics as much as cost savings, since not using a wood screw would force all sorts of janky design changes inside the drawer itself.
The finished product, or at least one small part of an ongoing project finished! Not bad for a lazy weekend!
Also, bonus pictures of the labels glued underneath the drawer. National Cash Register included these bills of sale with every register they sold, and they're an excellent way to confirm the year of manufacture if the labels survive.
Beats the pants out of thermal paper. :)
While I had some time, I decided to take care of the marble coin tap as well. This is a relatively straightforward process that only took a half hour at most. Decades of cigarette and pipe smoke, dirt, oil, all that nastiness built up on the marble until it resembled a dirty fingernail. Blech.
After a few hits of degreaser to get the surface grime off, I went over the marble with progressively finer grades of wet sandpaper until I felt I had gotten out as much discoloration as I reasonably could. Like I said in a previous post, I didn't expect it to come out perfectly white and new looking. It cleaned up nicely and held onto what it wanted to hold onto. The surface is clean, and that's the important part. Once its installed on the finished machine, I think it'll shine even brighter. ^_^
Anyhoo, thanks for reading! I'm off to burn the rest of the weekend playing 7 Days to Die! I'll have more updates soon, but right now I'm gonna relaaax... :3
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse
